data.title: Fiserv: Avoiding Falling Knives
data.description:
Fiserv, Inc. (FISV) plummeted by almost 70% in 2025. This massive dip is explained by repeated earnings disappointments, slashed guidance, and the class-action lawsuit. Wall Street analysts believe that FISV is substantially undervalued, with a projected 36% potential upside. However, I prefer to stay away because the forward P/E ratio setup is weak, with no contraction expected over the next several years. Moreover, the 2023-2025 guidance roller-coaster together with earnings disappointment suggests that the management lacks ability to reliably forecast the company's financial performance.

Last but not least, there is a class-action lawsuit initiated by some of the company's investors, which means significant legal and reputational risks. Overall, I prefer to stay away from this falling knife and assign a Hold rating for FISV.

Fundamental Analysis

Fiserv provides information management and e-commerce systems for financial services companies. Among Fiserv's services are online bill payment, account processing, account management, mobile banking, and debit card transaction processing. There are two segments: Merchant Solutions and Financial Solutions, each generating approximately the same amount of revenue. According to the latest 10-Q form, approximately 84% of revenue is generated domestically, with the remainder generated internationally with a relatively wide international presence.

If we look at Fiserv's share price chart over the last five years, we can see that there was a big bull run from around $120 in late 2023 to almost $240 by February 2025. This share price success was explained by strong organic growth, margin expansion, and bullish guidance revisions. However, expectations grew too fast because the company consistently failed to deliver positive revenue surprises over several quarters since late 2023. The biggest problem was the Q3 2025 earnings release, when FISV delivered a large negative revenue surprise together with a disastrous EPS miss. It was actual EPS of $2.04 against $2.65 consensus forecast. The stock plunged from $126 closer to $60 after earnings, and this reaction was explained not only by a wide EPS miss but also by a significant guidance cut.

Fiserv's guidance for 2023-2024 initially indicated impressive growth, followed by a notable divergence in actual performance, and ultimately a significant cut in guidance. I am not making any allegations, but these sharp changes of the management's guidance are difficult to reconcile with the macroeconomic reality. Yes, there is vast macroeconomic uncertainty due to high-interest rates and increased tariffs. Fiserv's business depends on the economy's health because total payment volumes drive the company's earnings.

However, the uncertainty does not equal sharp changes in the U.S. economy's growth pace. The country's GDP is still growing confidently, while unemployment rate remains notably below long-term historical averages. Once again, I am not making any allegations. However, the fact that some FISV investors initiated class action lawsuit against Fiserv and certain managers. This shows that investors are really concerned, which could make it a long and costly legal battle. A class-action lawsuit is always a big risk because it can cause severe reputational and financial damage. This is the big reason why I do not want to invest in FISV.

Apart from substantial legal risks and long record of disappointing earnings, I also prefer to avoid Fiserv because the digital payments industry demonstrates weakness. This is evident from the recent downgrade from J.P. Morgan for both FISV and PayPal (PYPL), citing "slower market growth and fears of commoditization." Moreover, the U.S. economic growth is poised to slow down in 2026 as the unemployment rate is steadily climbing while the Fed remains in the "no rush mode" regarding interest rate cuts, which is a notable macro headwind for Fiserv and companies alike.

Valuation Analysis

As a result of the massive 2025 share price drop, Fiserv is currently a $36 billion company. Most of the valuation ratios are extremely low compared to FISV's historical averages and the sector median. On the other hand, low multiples are likely explained by the fact that with all the aforementioned challenges, Fiserv's forward P/E ratio is expected to remain stable-as-table over the next several years. It implies that there will be no significant changes in the company's EPS, meaning no bottom-line growth.

Besides no projected EPS growth at the moment, let's not forget FISV's weak earnings surprise performance. Past performance does not guarantee future results, but it significantly affects probability of future outperformance or underperformance. With a poor earnings surprise record, there are high chances that we will see EPS underperformance in the future as well. It is another bearish factor weighing on the P/E contraction potential.

Overall, valuation analysis demonstrates mixed outcomes. I think that currently low multiples are fair given the class-action lawsuit and no projected P/E ratio contraction for several years ahead.

Mitigating Factors

While I believe that FISV is cheap for a reason, Wall Street analysts tend to disagree with me. The average price target is $92, which implies a compelling 36% upside potential. I could have ignored consensus target price if FISV was a small, under-the-radar company. However, FISV is an S&P 500 company, meaning that it is extensively covered by dozens of Wall Street analysts. The fact that their average price target suggests massive upside might indicate that I am overestimating the legal risk or adverse effect on FISV's growth potential from the increasing unemployment rate in the U.S.

Moreover, for a company with a large customer base and industry-leading profitability, there is always probability of a rapid comeback. The aforementioned class-action lawsuit is a risk, but Fiserv's reputation appears to be still strong because the company recently teamed up with Visa (V) to advance agentic commerce. With its $675 billion market cap, Visa is the largest company in this industry, and partnership with this giant might offset Fiserv's reputational damage from the class-action lawsuit.

Conclusion

Without exaggeration, FISV is a falling knife as the stock lost 70% of its value in 2025, which is extremely rare for an S&P 500 company. I usually tend to stay away from falling knives because such massive sell-offs rarely occur with no fundamental reason. The class-action lawsuit is a big risk, and FISV's weak earnings record is another big reason to be cautious. Fiserv has its strong sides and advantages, but I think that positives and negatives offset each other as of today. Therefore, a Hold looks like the most suitable rating for FISV.

data.title: Fenix Gold Offers Deep Value As Rio2 Production Launches Into A Gold Rush
data.description:
Among factors most important to determine whether a gold stock is worthy of investment are: management competency, gold deposit size & longevity, and complexity of mine plan. Rio2 Limited (RIOFF) merits checks across the board. The stock trades at $2.16 per share with a market cap of $868 million. Once production begins in January, the stock should calibrate closer to net present value-(NPV). I calculate a NPV of ~$2 billion or $4.72 stock price using company provided feasibility report, while adjusting to a gold price assumption of $4,300 per ounce.

Management includes executive chairman Alex Black, whose team delivered terrific results with Rio Alto mining which bought and developed a $48 million mine in Peru eventually selling the company to Tahoe Resources for $1.1 billion.

Rio2 holds a lucrative appreciating asset - the 1.7 million ounce Fenix gold mine project located in Chile. There are 3.3 million additional ounces currently listed as measure and indicated for a total of ~5 million. The mine plan projects 91,000 ounces of gold annually for the first 12 years, and 1.3 million total over full life at 17 years.

The oxide ore deposit is among the 3 largest open pit designs currently under development in North & South America. While many open pit mines must grind through vast rock waste, Fenix mine is unique with a very low .85 strip ratio. This factor dramatically reduces operational costs as there is less excavation necessary to generate gold. Similar open pit mines at Marigold and Mesquite, encounter more than 3x waste to produce gold.

Note the gold recovery rate is projected at 75% and the gold price was set at $1,750/ounce in the 2023 study. Two years out, and gold prices (XAUUSD:CUR) are now at $4,500.

The mine projects to deliver 91,000 ounces of gold annually over the first 12 years, with cash costs at ~$1,237 per ounce. I chose a conservative gold price held flat at $4,300 per ounce to estimate a net present value. I believe the gold price will rise higher in 2026 based on central bank and investor demand for physical metal amidst declining purchasing power of fiat currencies-the dollar being most important.

Investors should be attracted to potential for significant cash flow that will accelerate in future years as operating costs are likely to remain relatively stable. The ore deposit is excavated, dumped on the leach pad and treated with a solution to produce gold.

Management anticipates a mine expansion that will eventually increase gold production up to ~250,000 ounces per year by increasing tonnage mined from 20,000 to 80,000 per day. This would require increased water supply emanating via a pipeline from a desalination plant. This expansion would likely occur in a few years, and the cost likely to be shared by a neighboring mine operators such as Kinross. Rio2 expects to receive cost estimates in the first quarter.

Additional gold production via expansion would obviously drive this stock price much higher pending the expansion study results. The costs to expand are expected to be moderate considering the favorable dynamics with a heap leach oxide mining operation with an open pit. As more ore is added, the additional production of gold ounces tends to drive cash flow higher than costs. The water pipeline will be key to driving an expanded processing rate and Rio2 is engaged with neighboring mine operators who would benefit from a partnership.

Acquisition of Condestable mine

Rio2 recently announced the purchase the Condestable copper mine from Southern Peaks mining for $241 million in early December.

This acquisition appears to add two significant benefits. Most importantly the mine adds immediate ~$100 million in cash flow with EBITDA projected at $145 million. This copper producing mine will support the expansion costs for both Fenix and Condestable mines. Rio2 will add 80,000 gold equivalent ounces (GEO’s) annually with this transaction expected to close in late January, with an AISC of ~$2,100 per ounce. Free cash flow would likely provide a higher stock rating to Rio2 as a two asset miner with expansion studies well underway at both mines. A two asset mine is considered more attractive against risk events.

Fenix gold project is most sensitive to operating costs and gold price. Naturally a lower gold price moves the NPV down, but the feasibility study shows a robust mine even at $1,750 per ounce. Investing in a low cost gold mine with significant 5 million ounce potential total that scales sharply amid a gold renaissance presents a competitive buy opportunity. Do your own diligence to determine if Rio2 deserves a part of your portfolio.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: SentinelOne: Data And Cloud Should Drive A Stronger 2026
data.description:
SentinelOne's (S) stock has come under significant pressure in 2025, despite a seemingly modest valuation and a likely growth rebound in coming quarters. Net new ARR and RPO growth are accelerating, and SentinelOne's margins continue to improve. Some of this is likely related to the demand environment, which appears to have improved, although it remains variable across products and geographies.

I previously suggested that market conditions were responsible for SentinelOne's poor first-quarter results. The stock is down over 15% since then, which could be due to concerns about competitive positioning. This is highlighted by the fact that expectations of a growth rebound following the CrowdStrike outage fell flat. In addition, CrowdStrike (CRWD) appears to be making headway in the MSSP space.

While SentinelOne is still not without risk, its valuation is approaching a point where returns should be solid, absent a complete collapse in growth. I also tend to think that SentinelOne could become an acquisition target if its share price continues to fall.

Market Conditions

SentinelOne estimates its TAM will be more than $100 billion in 2025. Around half of this lies in areas like identity security, exposure management, and data protection, where SentinelOne currently has less of a presence. The core endpoint market is worth more like $20 billion, and the growth of next-gen vendors has decelerated significantly over the past few years. As a result, it is becoming increasingly important that SentinelOne's expansion into areas like cloud security, identity security, and SIEM are successful.

The cybersecurity demand environment appears to have strengthened in recent quarters, with cybersecurity company revenue beats rebounding modestly. This is supported by a reacceleration in cloud hyperscaler revenue growth and a modest improvement in white-collar employment growth.

Given developments like DOGE and the Federal government shutdown, it would be reasonable to expect Federal government spending to be an area of weakness. The public sector is a small part of SentinelOne's business, though, and it has performed in line with expectations so far.

Potentially one of the biggest factors impacting SentinelOne's stock currently is that CrowdStrike has partnered with Kroll. SentinelOne and Kroll partnered earlier in the year, with its platform powering the Kroll Responder MDR service. This appears to have been a competitive displacement, and CrowdStrike's management has suggested that this won't be a one-time occurrence. SentinelOne was fairly dismissive of the event, suggesting it hasn't been meaningfully impacted and that its partner ecosystem remains robust.

SentinelOne Business Updates

Focus areas for SentinelOne include:

  • Automation
  • Purple AI
  • AI SIEM
  • GenAI Security

This is important as the company's emerging products portfolio across SIEM/data, cloud, and Purple AI are growth drivers at the moment. In the third quarter of FY24, SentinelOne’s emerging solutions were responsible for something like $200 million ARR, with Cloud ARR sitting at around $100 million and Data ARR totaling roughly $70 million. SentinelOne's non-endpoint solutions were responsible for around 50% of bookings in Q3 FY25. Data bookings growth accelerated in the quarter, reaching a triple-digit YoY growth rate. SentinelOne's data solutions exceeded $100 million ARR in the first quarter, meaning something like a third of the company's growth is coming from its data solutions.

SentinelOne has strengthened its SIEM capabilities with the recent acquisition of Observo AI for approximately $225 million. Observo AI has a data pipeline solution, which is important for security operations and will help SentinelOne to capitalize on the legacy SIEM replacement opportunity. SentinelOne has suggested it will bring migration timelines down from months to weeks. Observo data pipelines are used to ingest and filter data before it reaches the SIEM or data lake. This cuts volumes by up to 80%, helping customers to reduce resolution times and cut costs by as much as 50%. Cribl has historically been the market leader, but it is designed for more structured data pipelines, whereas Observo can move data with non-structured schemas (useful for SIEM migration projects).

SentinelOne also recently acquired Prompt Security, a vendor focused on providing runtime security for GenAI (LLMs, AI assistants, agentic tools). Prompt Security's solution provides visibility, governance, and control, helping to prevent data leakage. The customer response to Prompt Security has reportedly been strong, which is not surprising given that it fills an important gap in the market.

Purple AI bookings were up by a triple-digit percentage YoY in Q1, and the product had more than a 25% attach rate across subscriptions sold in the quarter. The Purple AI attach rate reached 40% in the third quarter, up from 30% in the prior quarter. Purple AI supports security operations through automation and a natural language interface, which lowers barriers to adoption. Newer Purple capabilities include:

  • Natural language threat hunting
  • Agentic investigations
  • Automated response workflows

According to an IDC study, the Purple AI leads to a:

  • 60% reduction in probability of a majority security event
  • 55% faster threat remediation
  • Triple-digit 3-year ROI

Q3 was SentinelOne's strongest quarter for Cloud Security bookings growth in 12 months, which could be due to the introduction of a unified cloud security suite in the first quarter. SentinelOne's unified cloud solution was enabled by the acquisition of PingSafe in 2024 for its cloud-native application protection platform. AI workloads are likely to ensure that cloud security growth remains strong going forward. The importance of this part of the market is highlighted by Google's decision to acquire Wiz for approximately $32 billion. Wiz was hoping to reach $1 billion ARR in 2025, growing at a strong triple-digit rate.

Identity security is another important part of SentinelOne's business, due in part to the expected proliferation of agents. SentinelOne acquired Attivo for $617 million several years ago to expand its business into identity security. Attivo's identity protection is an agent-based solution that secures credentials and detects malicious identity behaviors. Attivo also offers identity infrastructure assessment, identity-based vulnerability scanning and management for enterprise infrastructure. At the end of 2021, Attivo's ARR was approximately 30 million USD and growing at something like a 50% rate. This is now a little-discussed part of the business, though, and it appears that SentinelOne may have overpaid.

Financial Analysis

SentinelOne generated $259 million in revenue in the third quarter, an increase of 23% YoY. While international revenue was up 34%, growth in the United States was only around 15%.

Net new ARR was $54 million, with ARR increasing 23% YoY. RPO was $1.3 billion at the end of the third quarter, an increase of 35% YoY. Flex is helping SentinelOne to land larger and longer-term deals, which is supporting growth of this metric. Roughly 50% of bookings came from emerging products in the quarter, with Singularity Data growing at a triple-digit rate.

SentinelOne's $100,000+ ARR customer cohort increased 20% YoY in the quarter, reaching 1,572. The company's net retention rate has also reportedly been stable in recent quarters. In terms of platform adoption, roughly 40% of enterprise customers have adopted 3 or more solution categories, and around 20% of enterprise customers have adopted 4 or more solution categories. These figures don't appear to be increasing that quickly, though, which is important given that multi-product adoption is closely related to churn. While it is difficult to compare, close to half of CrowdStrike’s customers have adopted 6 or more modules.

SentinelOne expects $271 million in revenue in Q4, representing an increase of 20% YoY. In comparison, CrowdStrike is guiding to 22% growth. Assuming a typical revenue beat, SentinelOne's growth should remain fairly stable in Q4, but is likely to accelerate meaningfully in the first half of FY27 due to SentinelOne lapping easier comparable periods. Both the Observo AI and Prompt Security transactions closed in the third quarter but will have minimal financial impact in FY26.

SentinelOne's margins continue to improve as the business scales and its growth decelerates. In particular, the burden of sales and marketing continues to decline, which is a positive signal of the health of the business and likely long-term margins. SentinelOne's free cash flow margin was 6% in the third quarter, and the company expects to be free cash flow positive for the full year.

Conclusion

SentinelOne's business has stabilized in recent quarters, and it appears set to reaccelerate as it begins to lap easier comparable periods. Large customer growth remains solid, and SentinelOne's emerging products are supporting revenue growth. An argument can also be made that AI-enabled attacks will be supportive of SentinelOne's automated approach to security.

SentinelOne's revenue multiple now sits well below leading cybersecurity peers, which can't really be attributed to its financial performance. As a result, it is hard not to feel that there is deep investor skepticism about SentinelOne's competitive positioning longer term. In this regard, SentinelOne's failure to capitalize on CrowdStrike's outage is concerning, as is CrowdStrike's recent MSSP win. I can't see SentinelOne's stock dropping too much further before becoming a takeover target, though.

SentinelOne is not without risk, including macroeconomic conditions, deal timing, and federal spending uncertainty. The company's CFO also recently left, adding to investor doubts. In addition, SentinelOne's Lenovo partnership and FedRAMP High authorization should have been a tailwind over the past 12 months. The fact that SentinelOne's growth has deteriorated against this backdrop could suggest the business is struggling.

While I believe that SentinelOne's valuation has now dropped to a point that returns will likely improve at some point in the near future, SentinelOne will need to stabilize its revenue growth and move closer to GAAP breakeven before this happens, which should occur in the first half of FY27. I rate shares a "Buy."

data.title: Figure Technology Solutions: Still Has Plenty Of Upside Left
data.description:
Summary

I gave a buy rating to Figure Technology Solutions (FIGR) before, with my key thesis being that FIGR has a structurally better solution to originate loans, one that is supported by its proprietary blockchain. Two months later today, the pivot to Figure Connect is driving real margin inflection, balance sheet risk is coming down, and adoption is accelerating faster than I expected. At the same time, FIGR is quietly expanding its addressable market beyond traditional HELOCs.

Figure Connect is doing its job

When I last wrote about FIGR, one of my key views was that the pivot to Figure Connect (a capital-light model) would derisk its balance sheet and allow margins to scale. At that time, my confidence level was not super high because we could only rely on early adoption traction. But today, I think we have a strong data point to prove that margins can indeed inflect. In the most recent quarter (Q3 2025), adj. EBITDA margin went up more than 10 points to 55.4% (vs. 44.9% in Q3 2024).

As for adoption, it was even more surprising. Since launching in June 2024, the platform has facilitated ~$2.4 billion in HELOC volume through September 2025. Remember that as of 1H25, the platform only processed ~$1.3 billion in loan volume (the first year). Which means in Q3 alone, FIGR processed $1.1 billion of loans, which is close to the entire first-year record. This tells me that the pace of adoption is accelerating, and the beauty is none of them requires FIGR’s capital. At the current pace, we are talking about FIGR potentially processing close to $4.5 billion of loans (and it is still growing), of which FIGR earns a high-margin transaction fee. If this momentum continues, it gives confidence that FIGR can hit management’s long-term EBITDA margin target of >60%.

Redefining the TAM

One of the biggest risks I highlighted back in October was product concentration. My view was that FIGR was heavily exposed to HELOCs, which meant FIGR’s performance was still linked to the housing cycle. I no longer think this is a big risk. While HELOCs still account for 99% of originations, my view has changed because I underestimated how flexible this product really is. FIGR is not just competing in home equity lending. It is also able to capture demand from refinancing.

This is a very interesting dynamic that expands on what TAM FIGR addresses. What’s happening here is known as the “first-lien HELOC” use case. Instead of drawing equity for renovations or discretionary spending, borrowers are using FIGR’s HELOC to pay off their existing mortgage entirely. This is a much bigger market than I thought. To put things in perspective, based on the Nasdaq London Investor Conference and the GS Financial Conference, this use case is ~20% of total volume.

I do believe FIGR can continue to capture share in this part of the market because FIGR's cost advantage works here as well. Like I have said before, traditional mortgage refinances are slow and expensive, often taking more than 40 days to close. They rely on paper-heavy processes and multiple handoffs, which drive up both time and cost. For recap, FIGR operates differently. FIGR records transactions on the Provenance blockchain and automates a significant portion of the workflow, driving down processing days to ~10 days. For the borrower, it makes a lot of sense to use FIGR since they can get the money faster.

This “new” TAM angle also means that even if discretionary spending slows and the traditional HELOC (for new homes) disappears, FIGR still has a durable source of growth coming from the need to refinance mortgage debt.

The "Blue Ocean" Call Option

What happened was that in November, FIGR filed an S-1 to register a new class of blockchain-native equity, in which the important part is the “Democratized Prime.” The simplest way to think about it is this. The way public equity is being handled in the market today is that it just sits idle in brokerage accounts. From time to time brokers may lend it out, but for the owner, there really isn’t much of an upside during this idle period (returns not related to the value of the equity). FIGR’s proposal changes that dynamic in a good way. By issuing equity natively on the blockchain, it means that the asset itself can be used in real time (i.e., when used as collateral to borrow via stablecoins or as lendable inventory that earns yield).

This upside potential is huge because the addressable market is larger than the lending market. Per the S-1 filing, the market for asset tokenization is expected to reach $16 trillion by 2030. If FIGR captures even a fraction of this volume, the revenue implications are massive. Even with a modest 50 bps take rate, the revenue opportunity for asset tokenization alone could be >$80 billion. For context, FIGR's total net revenue for the 9M 2025 was $347 million. We are talking about a revenue potential that is orders of magnitude larger than their current business.

The interesting part here is the optionality this creates. If this platform launches successfully, FIGR is no longer just a capital-light lender or a loan marketplace. It effectively becomes a hybrid of an exchange, clearing system, and custodian for tokenized financial assets. I believe this warrants a massive re-rating. For comparison, lenders typically trade at single-digit earnings multiples (e.g., Synchrony Financial, OneMain Holdings, and PennyMac Financial Services), but financial market infrastructure (like exchanges) trades at much higher multiples (think MSCI, S&P Global, Intercontinental Exchange, etc.). Also, unlike new crypto startups, FIGR already holds the necessary Alternative Trading System (ATS) and broker-dealer licenses and has already processed more than $60 billion in transactions on the Provenance Blockchain. As such, there are fewer hurdles to clear, de-risking the path ahead.

There is also a cost angle that increases the probability of success. Management noted that using the Provenance Blockchain has reduced third-party due diligence costs by ~80% because counterparties can rely on trusted data. This, then, creates a self-reinforcing loop. Lower operating friction should attract more partners to Figure Connect, which increases liquidity, which makes the ecosystem more useful, pulling in even more participants.

To be clear, regulatory approval for tokenized equity is not guaranteed, and this initiative should not be underwritten as the base case.

Valuation

The pace of Figure Connect adoption, a larger TAM, and the optionality tied to the “Democratized Prime” led me to upgrade my growth assumptions. The way I did it was to increase the exit year growth rate to 25% (it was 20% previously). As for valuations, I have upgraded to 14x forward revenue. I don’t think a compression to 10x would make sense since FIGR is evidently growing well, its products are working, processed loans from Figure Connect are scaling very quickly, and the “Democratized Prime” could drive a huge upwards multiples re-rating. Together, this led me to upgrade my share price target to $78.

Investment Risk

Although Figure Connect reduces balance sheet risk, it increases the risk of dependence since the model relies entirely on third-party liquidity. If institutional buyers on Figure Connect stop taking risks (i.e., don’t lend), growth could come to a halt. Furthermore, the "Democratized Prime" upside is contingent on regulatory approvals. If the SEC blocks the tokenized equity S-1, that "call option" expires worthless.

Conclusion

I maintain a buy rating on FIGR. Figure Connect is scaling faster than expected, margins are inflecting, and the business is becoming less exposed to housing cyclicality. On top of that, the “Democratized Prime” initiative introduces upside optionality that is not reflected in current valuation. If all things go well, in my view, FIGR is going to transition from a fintech into a broader financial infrastructure platform.

data.title: Tiernan Gold: My No. 1 Gold Idea For 2026
data.description:
Introduction

Last week was the first trading day for Tiernan Gold (TNGD:CA), which completed its IPO at a C$240M valuation. The company was structured around an asset from Hochschild Mining (HCHDF), which remains the company’s controlling and majority shareholder. In this article, I will explain why I like the asset, the company’s cashed-up balance sheet, and why the current market cap of around C$300M (at the current C$6 share price) still represents an attractive valuation.

As this is a recent IPO, you may ignore the share price chart preceding last Friday. The company only listed last Friday, and all data from before the first trading day is obsolete. The average daily volume in the first three days of trading was approximately 130,000 shares per day, for a monetary value of almost C$1M.

The capital structure

The company went public with a total share count of 48 million shares outstanding and 5.8 million warrants. Each full warrant allows the warrant owner to purchase an additional share at C$6.50 until November 2027. Completing the IPO financing means the company comes out of the gates with in excess of 50 million Canadian dollars in cash, which will go a long way to complete the pre-feasibility study and work toward a definitive feasibility study in the next few years.

Although Hochschild cashed in a little bit on the going public transaction (part of the C$65M IPO was a secondary offering by Hochschild), it still retains 69% of the current shares and 62% on a fully diluted basis. This could both be seen as an opportunity and a risk. It could be an opportunity, as Hochschild could potentially be interested in retaining full control of the assets once it has reached additional development milestones.

But it's also a risk as Hochschild may want to sell additional shares, which could create pressure on the share price.

I participated in the IPO financing, but I would still be interested in adding to the position on additional weakness. The recently completed IPO was conducted by a consortium of brokers and banks with Canaccord Genuity (CF:CA) representing 40% of the book, Bank of Montreal (BMO), a US$100B market cap bank, was co-lead agent and placed 30% of the book and Raymond James Financial (RJF) represented 20% of the deal size with one smaller Canadian firm placed 10% of the stock. Fortunately, I had an account with Canaccord Genuity, and I was able to secure a position in the IPO financing through that firm after my broker reached out to me.

Why I like the current valuation

There are three important reasons why I like the company at the current valuation. First of all, the project comes with permitted water rights with a total maximum pumping rate of 170 liters per second. An independent estimate of the usefulness of these water wells resulted in an anticipated 30 year useful life at an average of 124 liters per second. According to the preliminary economic assessment -completed by the company’s independent consultants, so not an in-house study - the current proposed mining scenario would only require 105 liters of water per second. It goes without saying that having access to water is a very important part of creating value on this project, as the lack of access to water certainly could be a deal-stopper in Chile.

Secondly, this is not a new discovery, as the current resource as well as the preliminary economic assessment were based on in excess of 150,000 meters of drilling.

And finally, as part of its process to get listed, the company went through all the required steps to provide the most up-to-date review of the project. While the most recent economic study is "just" a preliminary economic assessment, and we, of of course, will have to wait for a pre-feasibility study or a feasibility study, the PEA already provides an interesting and good look under the hood.

The economic study is based on an anticipated mining rate of 60,000 tons per day, and as per the preliminary economic assessment, the project could be built for a total of US$1.02B. As you can see below, list already includes a total contingency of approximately 25% (the capex excluding contingency is US$815M).

Based on that initial investment, the mine could be in operation for just under 14 years, producing an average of approximately 280,000 ounces of gold per year. This includes the final few years of the mine life where the production is tailing off, considering the average output in the first ten years of the mine life will exceed 330,000 ounces of gold.

As the breakdown above shows, the anticipated all-in sustaining costs per produced ounce of gold come in just below $1,100. While the copper price is currently slightly higher than the $4.5 per pound used in the economic study, the total copper output is quite low, and a higher or lower copper price will not move the needle by that much.

Using an average realized gold price of $2400 per oz, the after-tax NPV at 5% is estimated to come in at 1.5 billion USD, while the after tax internal rate of return would be almost 29%.

It goes without saying that's an excellent result, but now the gold price is consistently trading above $4,000 per oz., and considering we have seen recent economic studies using a gold price around $3000 per ounce, I think the sensitivity table shown below could also be very interesting. As you can see, applying an average gold price of $3,000 per oz would boost the after tax NPV by in excess of 50% to 2.36 billion USD.

Even if you were to apply a 25% higher capex in combination with a 25% higher opex, the after tax NPV at 5% at 3000 gold would still be around US$1.75B (rounded down), or C$2.5B using today’s exchange rates. So while a preliminary economic assessment obviously does not carry the same weight as a pre-feasibility study or feasibility study, it clearly shows there appears to be a lot of upside potential.

And while the sensitivity table maxes out at $3,600 per ounce, it's clear potential upside is tremendous should the gold price remain above $4,000/oz.

We should see the results of a pre feasibility study by the end of next year, and I think that will be one of the many opportunities for the company's share price to re-rate based on the increased confidence in the economic scenario.

Management

The incoming CEO of the company is Fausto Di Trapani, whose most recent employment was with MAG Silver (MAG) where he served as chief financial officer before the company was acquired by Pan American Silver (PAAS). While this is his first job as a chief executive officer, I'm assuming he has plenty of experience dealing with the developments of mining projects.

The board also includes Chris Taylor, the former CEO of Great Bear Resources, which was sold to Kinross Gold (KGC) (K:CA) in 2021 for C$1.8B.

These are real, experienced people, running a new company with an asset containing in excess of 10 million ounces of gold.

Risks associated with the investment thesis

While it's rare to see a multi-million ounce gold asset with existing water rights, an experienced management and board, and the support of its largest shareholder, which is a multibillion-dollar market cap gold and silver producer, there of course always are risks involved in this thesis.

I think the main risk is the gold price. It goes without saying that if the gold price were to drop by, for instance, 50%, the economics of the project wouldn't look nearly as good as they do in the base case scenario using $2,400 gold or the more optimistic $3,000 gold scenario.

While the initial treasury of in excess of 50 million CAD should last the company for the next little while, keep in mind this is a non-revenue company, and it will have to issue more shares to get the project through the feasibility study stage, and it will have to raise money to fund the potential construction of the asset.

And a final risk is, of course, dealing with Chile as a geopolitical risk as well as having a shareholder with a majority stake in the company. I'm not too worried about Chile, as the recent presidential election results in a win for the right-wing candidate. And I assume that Hochschild would want to maximize the value of its stake in Tiernan Gold. Rather than destroying value and tarnishing its own reputation.

The main risk I see is timing risk. As shown above, it would be unrealistic to expect the company to make a construction decision before the second half of 2029, and this will likely be delayed into 2030. But as far as a development stage asset goes, the potential benefits definitely outweigh the risks.

Investment thesis

Applying a multiple of 0.2 times the after tax net present value using 3000 gold and assuming a 25% higher CapEx and OpEx, the fair value of the projects would be approximately 500 million CAD today. As there are 48 million shares outstanding, this would result in a share price exceeding 10 CAD per share, and I think it is fair to use that as a very first target. As the project advances toward the pre-feasibility stage, it's only reasonable to expect to increase the multiple as the project gets de-risked from an economical and technical point of view.

I participated in the IPO financing, but I would still be interested in adding to the position on additional weakness. It doesn't happen very often that you can pick up a pre feasibility stage asset at just 0.10 times the after-tax NPV5% at $3000 gold (and including cost inflation).
