data.title: BSV: Balanced Treasuries + IG Credit In The Sweet Spot Of The Curve
data.description:
Intro & Thesis

In my last article on the Vanguard Short-Term Bond ETF (BSV), I mentioned that my position on this ETF was to hold because the market had already discounted part of the fall in interest rates in the 1-5 year range and, as a result, the fund's potential for price appreciation was more limited and asymmetrical. Today, with the Fed's December 10 cuts now in place, we have a basis for changing the ETF's rating because its main attributes make it an attractive product for the defensive fixed-income portion of an investment portfolio.

From here on, I believe that the expected return on the ETF should be based less on price and more on carry and roll-down, with a sensitivity to rates that is typical of the short end. I believe that carry will remain competitive compared to other alternatives with longer or shorter durations, as long as there are no tensions in credit spreads. This gives the vehicle under analysis sufficient attributes to raise the thesis from Hold to Buy, as in my opinion it has a favorable balance between the current income it provides and sensitivity to interest rates.

The ETF is passively managed, replicating the Bloomberg U.S. 1–5 Year Government/Credit Float Adjusted Index, which consists mainly of U.S. government debt and investment-grade corporate bonds with maturities between 1 and 5 years. The technique used to replicate the characteristics and performance of the index is representative sampling, whereby the index portfolio is replicated by purchasing a basket of bonds similar to that held by the index itself.

ETF Overview

In the first chart, we can see a comparison between the ETF's NAV performance and its market price performance, as well as the performance of the benchmark it seeks to replicate. The first comparison (NAV vs. Price) serves to see if it is an ETF that trades at a premium or discount to NAV over time. As can be seen in the image, the differences are minimal, especially when comparing long-term returns. In terms of index replication, there are hardly any differences in returns, so we can say that it achieves an almost perfect replication of the index. BSV has an expense ratio of 0.03% per annum, which compared to 0.61% for similar funds makes it particularly attractive in terms of fees (this is partly due to its passive management). From a carry perspective, the fund has distributed a dividend of 3.84% this year. That is why I say that it provides a decent level of carry, which will be partially affected by the fall in interest rates.

The Holdings

The main characteristics of the fund's portfolio are that it has 3,017 debt securities, compared to 3,900 in the benchmark it seeks to replicate. The average duration and effective maturity of both the ETF and the benchmark are at the same level: 2.8 and 2.6 years, respectively. This confirms what was mentioned earlier in the thesis. The ETF has a short duration, which means it is not overly sensitive to interest rates, but not so short that it fails to capture the rise in the price of the bonds in its portfolio when interest rates fall. This occurs because, unlike an ultra-short duration ETF, its duration allows it to hold securities in the portfolio for longer, without the need to roll down positions. For this reason, I believe it has more potential than an ultra-short fund and less risk than a long-duration fund, which also carries the risk of an increase in the term premium.

If we look at the details of the ETF portfolio, we can see why I like it and why it has a good balance. In the image showing the distribution by credit quality, we can see that 70.32% of the portfolio is invested in US government bonds, followed by almost 30% in investment-grade corporate bonds, weighted between A (12%) and BBB (11.60%) ratings. In my opinion, this provides a balance that other vehicles do not have, given the current macroeconomic environment.

The maturity distribution chart shows how the portfolio weight is distributed among the following maturities: 28.66% between 1 and 2 years, 26.11% between 2 and 3 years, 21.42% between 3 and 4 years, and 19.77% between 4 and 5 years, making up approximately 95% of the total portfolio. With this distribution of maturities, the average duration is 2.6 years, which provides flexibility in that it does not lose much in carry with the fall in interest rates and gains in price due to the appreciation of the bonds in the portfolio.

Macro Overlay

To further justify the upgrade of the thesis, I propose the following metrics: 2-year zero-coupon and the adjusted indicator on financial conditions produced by the Federal Reserve Bank of Chicago. In the first chart (zero-coupon), we can see how the yield on the 2-year bond is very close to the average of expected short-term rates for the next two years. This indicates that the short end of the curve (where BSV resides) is pricing in the Fed's script via expectations, and that the additional upside margin from IRR compression is more limited unless the cuts end up being faster or deeper than the market currently discounts. This supports the proposed buy thesis.

If we look at the second chart, we can see that the credit and leverage component is at moderate levels, which indicates that corporate credit is under control and shows no signs of widening spreads. Therefore, I recommend buying, as I believe it is a vehicle that will perform better than cash plus ultra short. Thank you for reading.

data.title: Trilogy Metals: Solid Macro Tailwinds But Wait For Execution
data.description:
Introduction And Investment Thesis

I am optimistic about Trilogy Metals Inc. (NYSE: TMQ) but rate it a hold driven by the permit granted for access to the Ambler Project, which enables the development of critical metals in Alaska. Despite the promising news, the price return has retreated, a sign of cautious market sentiments after a strong rally. One way I am looking at this is that a vital permit is necessary because the Biden Administration had denied access to the Ambler Road, limiting access to Alaska mining. Now, my interest is in looking at the gains after the U.S. grants this permit.

For example, Trilogy Metals holds 50% of Ambler Metal, which manages the Upper Kobuk Mineral Project (UKMP) in Alaska, meaning that Trilogy’s share gives it equal control and economic interest in South32. Of much importance here is that the U.S. federal government, after granting this access to UKMP, has gone ahead to invest around $35.6 million for this critical metal resource through the Department of War and holding a 10% stake in Trilogy Metals.

Leveraging the permit granted to access the Ambler Project, Trilogy has now approved a budget of around $35 million for 2026, intended for the mining permit. The Arctic resource has around 46.700 million tonnes of total concentrates, where 3.7% of this comprises copper. There are other minerals, such as cobalt, which I will cover later. The picture I am drawing here is that the U.S. is currently considering rare earths, copper, and lithium as critical metals as part of assets under national security that cannot be left to the whims of the open market.

Another dimension of this optimism is looking at the Trilogy price return. After getting the permit to access the Ambler Project, the price return gained significantly, from 93% to 880%, beating the S&P 500 by a distant margin. Nevertheless, the YTD price return has dropped to around 330.56% but is still beating the S&P 500, which is at 16.49% which shows that the market is possibly cautious about how things could turn out following the recent development.

Following the government backing, this company has indicated signs of vibrant performance, and I will dive into this explanation below, indicating why there is optimism, but I will also include why this company warrants a Hold rating.

Company Brief

Trilogy Metals Inc. explores mineral properties in the United States. The company focuses on copper, cobalt, lead, zinc, gold, and silver. The principal asset is the Upper Kobuk Mineral Project (UKMP) in Alaska’s Ambler mining district, which spans around 448,217 acres. The mining resources include the Arctic deposit, which is a polymetallic volcanogenic massive sulfide resource, and the Bornite deposit. This is a carbonate-hosted copper-cobalt resource. The company was incorporated in 2004 and is headquartered in Vancouver, Canada.

A Holistic View

Trilogy is currently surrounded by an external tailwind, which I am attaching to the U.S. federal government backing. This support has started on a high note with the permit granting Trilogy access to Ambler Road, and now I am looking forward to the mining permit, estimated to cost $35 million. But the question here is why this support. In my findings and answer, I will begin by drawing you closer to the recent action taken by the U.S., considering copper as an asset requiring national security. The intention is to have 25% of copper input materials produced in the U.S. by 2027, 30% by 2028, and 40% by 2029.

There is an expected high demand for copper in the next 10 years, between 2025 and 2035, driven by artificial intelligence (AI) and data centers. Copper will be used to supply electricity, and data centers will be consuming a lot of electricity. With the U.S. expected to lead in the growth of data centers globally, the electricity demand is expected to hit 80GW by 2030, up from 25GW in 2024. As such, 1.5 million mt of copper will be demanded, which is why copper is on the list of national security assets.

Even further, the U.S. government has recently updated its list of critical metals and has included four minerals of interest to Trilogy, which include zinc, copper, cobalt, and germanium. Recall that data centers and AI are expected to grow rapidly, with the U.S. as the lead. Now, these minerals are a major contributor to the ecosystem that facilitates this growth. For example, germanium is used in semiconductors essential for data centers, and cobalt is used in making energy storage technologies in electric vehicles (EVs).

From this picture, it is now easier to create a nexus between why Trilogy is experiencing volatility in its stock price immediately after the U.S. granted access to Ambler Road. I think the next volatility will now be backed by the commencement of mining, which, I think, is not too far in the future, after announcing the planned processing of the mining permit in 2026. Given that this company is U.S.-backed, I believe the permit may not take too long, as the demand for copper keeps rising.

The Arctic resource is high-grade, and this is one of the significant interests of the U.S. in maintaining its critical metals supply chain. With an overall 46.7 million tonnes of mineral reserves, this has rich concentrates. For example, copper concentrate has a high recovery level of 92.1% with a 30.33% target metal in the concentrate and a high payable percentage of 96.5%. The Arctic resource does not have penalty metals, which means that at this high percentage of concentrates, there are no contaminants that are likely to reduce the payable value rate.

For those who don’t know, the payable percentage is also essential because when it is high, it means that the mining resource has concentrates that result in a high chance of buyers paying more for the final concentrate after factoring out refining costs. In fact, this high payable rate is seen in the comparison of Trilogy Arctic resource against its peers, where its resource grade is at 5.40%, highest among its peers.

Financials And Valuation

This company is in a pre-revenue exploration stage, but it shows a high potential Internal Return Rate (IRR) of 23.6% pre-tax IRR and 20% after-tax IRR with a payback period of less than 5 years.

This revenue potential lies in the Arctic Project, which has completed a feasibility study. Given that, the annual production is estimated to be around 149 million pounds (67,626 tonnes) of copper and 173 million pounds (78,470 tonnes).

Assuming the mining permit is given in 2026, and 2027 mining kicks off, the price of copper is expected to range between $10,000 and $11,000 per metric tonne in 2026 to 2027. A midrange of this is $10,500 per metric tonne, which means that if Trilogy sells around 67,626 tonnes of copper, it is possible to make revenues in the region of $710.073 million in 2027. This aligns with the consensus revenue estimate, which does not include the revenue record estimate in 2026. With copper recording a larger percentage of concentrate in the Arctic resource, it means that the U.S. federal government support is likely to accelerate the realization of 62% of revenues from copper in the near-term, which I think initial mining might kick off in 2027. Adding revenues from other concentrates like zinc at 23%, the company has a high chance of a solid revenue base.

Right now, I am looking forward to the granting of the mining permit scheduled for 2026, which will bring back revenue rebound closer, led by copper sales.

On the bottom line, this company has not been profitable, which is expected because the company is still in the pre-revenue phase. However, since its inception in 2020, the trend shows that this company has the potential to become profitable. The net loss has narrowed from $21.66 million in 2021 to a net loss of $9.18 million, an indication of improving financial efficiency. While this being a loss-making company, financial sustainability is a critical point for investors, I find the company stable. As of Q3 2025, its total operating expenses for the past 9 months came in at $6.06 million, translating to an average of about $2.03 million per quarter. This translates to about $8.08 million per year. Dividing this by the available liquidity ($23.72 million in cash and ST investments) translates to a runway of about 2.9 years, which is a solid financial footing offering stability and flexibility.

In the November 2025 presentation, the profitability index indicates an IRR of around $1.108 billion after tax, which is around 22.8% IRR, representing a payback period of 3.1 years. This gives a proximate possible profitability in 2028, which aligns with projections if mining starts in 2027 and profitability is likely to kick off in 2028.

Moving to valuation, a Price/Book multiple to that of its peers, Santacruz Silver Mining Ltd. (Pink Current Info: SCZMD) and Compass Minerals International, Inc. (NYSE: CMP), indicates my inherent optimism. The P/B multiple of TMQ is 6.03x, the highest among the three companies.

Trilogy P/B multiple shows that it has high investor expectations tied especially to the Arctic project, which has a high production rate and IRR. The Trilogy Arctic project shows a huge potential rather than the current profitability, and this is why I think government support resulted in a significant YTD price return.

Investment Risk: Mining Permit Dependency

Right now, Trilogy is depending on a mining permit to be given after the permit to access the Ambler Access Road. The company remains pre-revenue with recurring losses and dependence on external funding. I think the subsequent grounded bullish reversal, in my opinion, is hinged on the Arctic and Bornite mining permit, which will result in the commencement of mining. Regulatory delays might erode these projects’ economics and increase execution risk, such as financing mining infrastructure.

Even further, Trilogy’s total cash and short-term investment is $23.37 million while its total operating expenses after 9 months in FY2025 as of Q3’2025 are $6.063 million, which leads to an approximate $8.084 million per year. This gives an approximate cash runway of 2.9 years, which means the available cash can sustain the company for around 3 years. Since it is likely that after the mining permit is issued in 2026, the commencement of mining might happen in 2027, any regulatory delay may constrain this company’s performance.

Conclusion

This is one of the companies that has a huge potential driven by the high concentration of reserves in its mining sites, which has driven the support of government backing. However, one thing remaining in this company is that it is still pre-revenue and, in my view, I see that it is a worthwhile ground for a hold stance until it proves it can exploit its potential.

data.title: Abbott Laboratories Shrugs Off Tariffs And China Headwinds With A 7% Dividend Raise
data.description:
Introduction

Over the past 2 or 3 years, I've watched several businesses see slowdowns due to higher for longer interest rates. And with tariff implementations, this has become more evident, particularly from businesses with operations in China.

Abbott Laboratories (ABT), despite margin pressures and lingering headwinds, has navigated successfully, displaying resilience.

Additionally, the Dividend King delivered a solid dividend raise earlier this month amid headwinds, which should give investors confidence going forward.

In this article, I discuss Abbott Laboratories' latest earnings, fundamentals, and why, despite their headwinds, I'm initiating a buy rating on the stock.

Brief Overview

As previously mentioned, Abbott Laboratories is a Dividend King. However, their history dates back to the 18th century when the company was founded by physician Dr. Wallace Abbott.

ABT is a medical device company that debuted on the stock market exchange in 1929 and has since become a household name.

They famously spun-off AbbVie (ABBV) in 2013 and currently operates in four segments: Medical Devices, Diagnostics, Nutritional Products, & Established Pharmaceutical Products. Their Medical Devices segment accounts for most of the company's sales.

They also operate in 160+ countries in Europe, Africa, Asia-Pacific, North America, & South America.

Latest Earnings Show Operational Resilience

Abbott Laboratories reported their Q3 earnings back in October and displayed resilience despite a mixed earnings report. Revenue of $11.37 billion missed analysts' estimates by $20 million, while EPS of $1.30 was in-line.

Shortly after, the stock sold off as management also narrowed their full-year outlook. EPS was narrowed to $5.12 - $5.18, down from $5.10 - $5.20. However, this still represents a double-digit growth rate of 10.28% from 2024's $4.67.

Through the first 3 quarters, EPS amounted to $3.65, up from $3.33 the year prior. Sales through 3 quarters were $32.87 billion, up from $30.98 billion. So, solid performance despite lingering China headwinds and tariff impacts.

ABT's performance was driven by double-digit growth in Medical Devices, which saw an increase of 12.5%.

Nutrition sales growth was 4%, while Established Pharmaceutical Products saw growth of 7%.

Diagnostics saw some growth, although this was modest as challenges in China weighed on the segment. These impacted both price & volumes.

Excluding China, growth was 7%. Recently launched products also contributed to ABT's performance, providing a sales boost of $0.5 billion.

These also added more than 100 basis points to organic sales growth, which amounted to 7.5%. Operating gross margins saw impacts from tariffs, down from 56.3% the previous year's quarter to 55.8%.

This is in comparison to peer Stryker Corp (SYK) whose gross margins were up 50 basis points to 65%. Intuitive Surgical (ISRG) also saw their margins decline year-over-year by 110 basis points to 68%.

Pristine Balance Sheet

Abbott's balance sheet was in phenomenal shape with an A credit rating and only $11.6 billion in long-term debt. This was minimal in comparison to their market cap of more than $217 billion. Liquidity was also robust with $7.5 billion in cash & cash equivalents and only $1.345 billion in short-term debt.

Positioning For Future Growth

Last month news broke of Abbott Laboratories acquiring cancer test maker Exact Sciences (EXAS) in an all-cash deal for $21 billion. The deal is expected to add roughly $1.8 billion in debt to ABT, but again this is minimal.

The transaction is expected to be completed by Q2 of 2026. If the deal gets approved, this will add to ABT's already large portfolio and will likely be accretive to earnings going forward.

Outlook

For the fiscal year, ABT is expecting high, single-digit sales growth and double-digit EPS growth. Me, personally, I think this is solid all things considered.

And with lower base rates likely to provide tailwinds and tariff impacts likely to dissipate in the next year or two, ABT could be poised to see double-digit top & bottom line growth in the near to medium-term.

7% Dividend Increase

Even with tariffs & China headwinds, Abbott Labs showed that their dividend track record is not in danger with a 7% dividend increase earlier this month. The $0.04 increase was in-line with the company's raises in the past 3 years.

This was also solid considering the tough economic backdrop, putting this well above the current inflation rate. Additionally, this was higher than peer Stryker's 4% dividend increase earlier this month.

ABT's dividend was backed by strong cash flows. During the quarter, the company paid $1.030 billion in dividends and $3.091 billion through the first 9 months. Cash from operations grew from $5.690 billion to $6.251 billion.

Subtracting their CAPEX of $1.482 billion, this gave ABT a comfortable payout ratio of 64.8%. This gives them plenty of financial flexibility for future acquisitions as well as retained capital to repurchase shares and drive earnings growth.

Still Appears Expensive, But Premium Is Warranted

Seeing by the company's resilience and business model of providing life-changing products, I'm not surprised the company trades at a premium despite headwinds. Using their guidance midpoint, ABT's forward P/E sits at 24.2x.

For comparison, Stryker's forward P/E is higher at 26.17x. While ABT's is higher than the sector median's forward P/E of close to 20x, theirs was in-line with their 5-year average.

While I do believe the company could see solid price appreciation in the coming years and that they warrant a premium, the margin of safety is minimal, as their 2027 price target is similar to the current price of $124.54.

Therefore, significant upside could be limited. Me, personally, I believe investors should consider loading up if the price falls close to the $100 level.

Risks

Abbott faces a few risks. One, the deal with Exact Sciences could face scrutiny and be denied. Although I don't foresee this, this could result in share price volatility. Another risk is the company could continue to see margin pressures.

If so, this could also cause the share price to sell-off as investors wait for sequential improvements. And lastly, the stock does appear to offer little to no margin of safety. If we see a steep market correction, ABT could face share price volatility as a result.

Conclusion

Abbott has performed well despite headwinds in China and impacts from tariffs. Moreover, their pending acquisition of Exact Sciences is likely to add solid growth over the long term.

Their fundamentals remain solid, with expectations to see high, single-digit growth in revenue and double-digit growth in their bottom line. The recent dividend increase is also a testament to their fundamentals, as this is well-covered by growing cash flows.

While I rate the stock a buy, investors should be aware that significant upside may be limited due to their stock's premium valuation. For those considering, watch for any signs of share price weakness and consider adding closer to the $100 share price level.

As a result of their resilient business, strong fundamentals, and likelihood to see tailwinds from lower base rates that could result in high growth in the near to medium-term, I believe Abbott Laboratories is a solid dividend growth idea worth owning long-term.

data.title: Eli Lilly: Mr. Market Finally Woke Up, But I'm Not Out Yet (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Selling your winners and holding your losers is like cutting the flowers and watering the weeds.

-Peter Lynch, One Up on Wall Street (1989)

Main thesis

At the end of July, I wrote a bullish article called 'Competition melts away', assigning a Strong Buy rating to Eli Lilly (LLY).

Since then, the company has delivered a ~40% return over just 5 months and significantly outperformed the S&P 500 and the broader industry:

The GLP-1 industry is very close to yet another regulatory stress test as oral versions and stage II injectables are set to get a series of FDA approvals. This may increase the competition threat to Lilly's tirzepatide. But the data still massively favours only one company, in my opinion.

And while I lower my rating, the thesis on Eli Lilly being the strongest pharma powerhouse out there stays true. The company continues to trade at <1x fwd PEG and maintains one of the highest growth rates in the market with exposure to the most important areas of the industry with slow and weak competition.

Tirzepatide won the Stage I battle

Lilly's tirzepatide (Mounjaro/Zepbound) recently surpassed Keytruda as the world's best-selling drug and now generates over $10 billion per quarter.

If you still don't understand why New Generation GLP-1/GIP receptor agonists became such a big deal so quickly for Lilly and the industry, this chart should illustrate how dependent the company is getting on just one formula:

In the last article, I assumed that recently published head-to-head data on tirzepatide vs Novo's semaglutide (NVO) suggested a significant weight loss advantage of tirzepatide in both obesity and T2D.

Lilly's drug accelerated its market share uptake in Q2-Q3'25 via both revenue and total prescription count and now accounts for ~52% of the total incretin market, despite not being accessible through an oral version (semaglutide has an approved oral version, Rybelsus):

As a result, Eli Lilly hiked its revenue growth guidance by 7%, while Novo Nordisk was forced to slash its targets by ~12%, compared to expectations set in Q1'25.

Tirzepatide remains the only approved medicine that leads to ~20% weight loss in non-T2D and ~15% in T2D adults within a 72-week period, superior to Novo's competitor and especially superior to old-gen GLP-1s, along with SGLT2 inhibitors and insulin glargine.

I believe a ~7 p.p. weight loss gap between Tirzepatide and Semaglutide should inevitably lead to an even wider performance gap and market share growth in stage I of the GLP-1 battle:

The race should get more competitive

The battle is far from over, though, and Lilly's positions are about to be stress-tested on at least two different levels.

Overall, I split the GLP-1 race into three main stages:

While tirzepatide and 'raw' semaglutide are certainly not going anywhere, I project their growth rates may start to phase out as early as next year, making space for new upcoming platforms. While there likely won't be a leap in weight loss comparable to stage I vs stage 0, new projects certainly appear more powerful now.

New GLP-1 combos are set to enter the market in 2026-2027. Current GLP-1 products target appetite and insulin suppression exclusively, but since the human body has multiple weight-defensive mechanisms, they run into tolerability and efficacy ceilings. Theoretically, if more weight loss pathways were carved through cocktail formulas, the early plateau and ~20% weight loss cap could be broken.

Stage IIa candidate № 1: CagriSema

CagriSema (semaglutide + cagrilintide) adds amylin receptors to the equation. Although it failed to achieve an internal target of 25% weight loss, CagriSema's phase 3 results were comparable with tirzepatide both in non-T2D and T2D. However, it came at the expense of higher muscle burn and harsher AEs.

Although it is a cross-trial comparison and we'll only see head-to-head results vs tirzepatide in early 2026 (REDEFINE-4), I think there is enough data to suggest that CagriSema will be taking on tirzepatide's market share via pricing pressure and Zepbound non-responders, despite a worse safety profile.

Stage IIa candidate № 2: Retatrutide

The whole different story is Lilly's Retatrutide. We have recently gotten results for the TRIUMPH-4 phase 3 trial in osteoarthritis, where the highest 12 mg dose showed a -23.7% weight loss (ITT regimen) in 68 weeks.

Doesn't really look like a big jump, but let's look closer at the data:

  1. This is a trial in osteoarthritis, meaning most patients are unable to perform cardio due to knee pain. Osteoarthritis was previously linked to ~1.2 p.p. lower weight loss in Semaglutide.
  2. Dropout was ~3x higher compared to tirzepatide trials, creating a huge ITT gap (5 p.p.).

This 5 p.p. weight loss ITT gap is unprecedented in GLP-1 trials, and we will have to dig deeper into these dropout rates.

Eli Lilly said in the press release that this gap included "perceived excessive weight loss" (i.e., some patients were losing weight too rapidly). We do have a signal on dropouts being as much as 6 p.p. lower with BMI≥35, reflecting the correlation of higher discontinuation rates with lower starting weight:

While we'll get the full picture once TRIUMPH-4 results are published in a journal in a couple of months, this indicates that the bariatric surgery floor of ~25% would be broken by Eli Lilly, at least in common obesity.

As for osteoarthritis, the only GLP-1 approved directly against it now is semaglutide, and we can only guess how many knee replacement procedures could be avoided with retatrutide.

Safety-wise, Reta showed at least non-inferior results to CagriSema in key adverse event categories, despite a significant gap in efficacy:

My thesis from the previous article stays the same: Novo Nordisk, along with Amgen (AMGN) and Pfizer (PFE), is not ready for stage IIa competition with Retatrutide and lacks any competitive advantage over Tirzepatide, while other firms are still too early in pipeline trials. Novo's fight for Metsera at 2x market price and ~$2B licensing deal for a Chinese triple agonist UBT251 only serve as a signal of weak competitive positioning, in my opinion.

However, CagriSema's NDA will be filed in early 2026, while we will only see Retatrutide phase 3 results in common obesity in the end of Q2'26, giving Novo more than a 2-quarter timing advantage.

'Good enough' tirzepatide alternatives should slow down Lilly's current double-digit revenue growth rates in 2026-2027, which is the main reason for a downgrade, while staying bullish.

In orals, Rybelsus ≈ Orforglipron

We have recently gotten data from ATTAIN trials, meaning we can now compare two oral agents: Lilly's Orforglipron with oral semaglutide.

In T2D, the winner is quite clear, as Orfo with 9.6% weight loss outperforms 6.7% Sema 25 mg, which can also be confirmed via network meta-analysis of ACHIEVE-3 (Orfo vs oral Sema 14 mg) and PIONEER PLUS (oral Sema 25 mg vs oral Sema 14 mg).

At the same time, the obesity trial came with significant design discrepancies (more male patients, 4x the percentage of Hispanic ethnicity, no kcal deficit required), but Orfo still lost in cross-trial in key comparison groups, although with a better safety profile:

Although it's very early to write off Lilly's pill in the non-T2D obesity market due to a lack of quality meta-analysis, Orfo and oral Sema theoretically should split the market 50/50 (Lilly takes T2D; Novo takes most of obesity).

I have high expectations for the oral GLP-1 agonist market, mainly for maintaining weight loss after injectable treatment. Pills might take some market share from injectables, but it would mostly be at 'raw' semaglutide's (stage I) expense, as tirzepatide's results remain unreachable for orals for now.

The FDA has recently approved a 25 mg oral semaglutide version, while Eli Lilly only submitted its NDA at the year-end. And although I've softened up my estimates on oral GLP-1 market domination, Lilly's oral candidate has every chance to beat Novo's competitor despite the timing advantage.

As Orforglipron is a small molecule (oral semaglutide is a peptide), it is designed to resist stomach acid and absorption barriers, meaning no fasting is needed, while Novo's drug comes with a 30-minute no-drink/water restriction.

On the manufacturing side, it is way easier to scale Orfo production. Due to its non-biological nature, Eli Lilly does not need recombinant fermentation plants, meaning almost any CDMO (third-party manufacturer) in the world can be hired for Orfo production. The company has also already made significant investments in the oral medicine facilities, making the Novo timing advantage short-lived.

Kisunla & Jaypirca gain traction

Alongside the GLP-1 portfolio, these two are huge secondary growth drivers for the company.

Kisunla remains the only Alzheimer's disease modifying therapy that doesn't require a lifelong treatment course. According to a recent network meta-analysis study, it is associated with a ~1.5x larger effect size on the Cognitive Scale CDR-SB compared to its only competitor, Biogen's (BIIB) Leqembi, though with a higher brain swelling and bleeding trade-off.

Kisunla was recently approved by EMA in Europe, which was the only region it lacked to catch up with Leqembi:

At least two drivers in 2026-2027 should move it closer to Street's consensus of $5 billion in peak sales:

  1. Remternetug (Kisunla for 'take-home' subcu injection) phase 3 ends in March 2026. Biogen has already submitted its subcutaneous version to the FDA.
  2. In May 2025, the FDA cleared Fujirebio's Alzheimer's blood detection test. With around 92% of expected mild cognitive impairment cases remaining undiagnosed, cheap blood tests should significantly accelerate Kisunla's TAM.

Moreover, Novo Nordisk and Johnson & Johnson (JNJ) flopped their Alzheimer's trials in November 2025 as both EVOKE and AUTONOMY studies failed to achieve statistical significance. No additional competition, therefore, is expected for at least the next two years.

As for Jaypirca, it remains the single most promising therapy in lymphocytic leukemia, in my opinion. It is currently only approved in L2+ (second line of treatment) CLL/SLL after covalent BTKi exposure and has a conditional approval in 3L+ in MCL.

The problem with the current CLL/SLL treatment is that we do have quite efficient covalent BTK inhibitors that create selective pressure on the tumor, leading to relapse in ~13-16% of patients (C481 mutations), therefore remaining resistant to covalent BTK inhibitors and AE intolerance in another ~20-25%.

Until recently, the only option for L2 after cBTKi was extremely toxic Venclexta, which requires a 5-week ramp-up with weekly clinic visits. Jaypirca has shown a significant reduction (>3x) in AEs with similar progression free survival rates (cross-trial comparison).

The released BRUIN CLL-314 phase III trial data, released in December, however, suggests that Jaypira also succeeds in 1L treatment and outperforms Imbruvica in h2h. Moreover, this trial showed better results than 1L leader Brukinsa did in its SEQUOIA trial (>2x lower hazard ratio; higher PFS):

The problem for L1 now is sequence: we do know that Jaypirca works after cBTKi, but we do not know what works after Jaypirca besides Venclexta (not enough data on L528W and T474 clones). Therefore, only a slow adoption of the L1 market is expected after approval.

However, this is just a matter of timing until the data matures and BTK degraders like BGB-16673 that solve Jaypirca's resistance problem hit the market for L2.

Significant toxicity and efficacy advantages, supported by data on trial dropout and HR, create strong ground for dominance across the whole CLL/SLL vertical in my opinion.

Possible strikes in Waldenstrom's and marginal zone lymphomas for Jaypirca are just additional drivers that would drive its sales at least to the level of BTKi peers post-2026:

Eli Lilly has a number of other promising projects across neuroscience and oncology, like the radiopharmaceutical portfolio acquired from Point Biopharma in 2023.

However, I estimate Incertins + Kisunla + Jaypirca to account for >80% of Lilly's $120B total sales in 2030 (Wall St consensus), meaning the investment thesis mainly lies in these three categories.

Lilly-Trump deal goes deeper than tariffs

At the end of July, the White House sent out dozens of letters to Big Pharma CEOs with similar content, threatening targeted price cuts in exchange for tariff exemptions:

Just a few months later, all visible names struck a deal by agreeing to offer selected products at a significantly lower cost via the government's D2C platform called 'TrumpRx'.

Lilly and Novo both agreed to offer Zepbound and Wegovy at a ~68% discount to the listing price of $1,100 (average of ~$350/month, depending on the dose). Sounds like a bad deal just to avoid political pressure.

However:

Firstly, only a negligible number of patients actually bring $1,100 net revenue per Zepbound. Leerink Partners estimates an average net realized price for Zepbound-covered commercial plans at $450-$550 per month. The 'uninsured' and 'denied' mostly rely on LillyDirect, where Zepbound vials (DIY syringes) sell for ~$400 on average, or just go to the compounding grey market via Hims & Hers (HIMS).

Secondly, this deal is not even about TrumpRx. As part of these negotiations, Zepbound and Wegovy are set to be covered by Medicare Part D and Medicaid as anti-obesity therapies at a $245 net realized price by mid-2026. The Social Security Act explicitly bans the government's coverage for weight loss drugs, meaning the only way to get Tirzepatide as a Medicare/Medicaid beneficiary now is via T2D or sleep apnea.

But with the new pilot 5-year GENEROUS Model, any patient with chronic obesity would get coverage post-2026.

Considering the ~40% obesity prevalence in US adults, that's a completely unpenetrated TAM of ~28 million patients (~$80 billion) for Eli Lilly for the taking, with 6.6 million rollout next year. This represents a massive volume growth driver, and it is way better to enter this market at a lower NRP than to miss the chance to penetrate it, in my opinion.

I do think that Lilly's performance margins may 'flatten out' in 2026 due to volume influx at lower NPR. But this is once again just an issue of timing, as even at the lower government prices ($149 starter / $346 maintenance), I estimate profit margins on coming oral Orforglipron to be much higher than the pen (no glass, no needle, and no cold-chain shipping), meaning the effect should be fully offset by 2027:

No longer a 'Strong Buy', but still cheap

As the company gets closer to a $1T market cap threshold, the current valuation still remains in the buy zone.

Obviously, we will look beyond the infamous forward P/E, but here is a good chance to highlight that pharma companies are not just valued based on today's profitability and future growth, but also based on the safety of the portfolio as a whole.

Post-LOE (loss of exclusivity) drug performance is incredibly hard to estimate, given the volatile nature of the competition, meaning the safety of the portfolio is the premium feature by itself:

Since the last article, the forward PEG ratio jumped from ~0.8x to ~1x, mainly due to 40% market cap growth and ATTAIN-1 lower-than-estimated results, partially offset by Retatrutide success and guidance hike.

As the closest competitor, Novo Nordisk (NVO) completely broke apart (forward PEG > 2x), I am comparing LLY's PEG with other companies with double-digit/high single-digit EPS growth:

Despite the market noticing and closing a better part of the discount, Eli Lilly remains the cheapest in the group, based on the growth rates, on par with AbbVie. I link this to prevailing competition fears in the Stage IIa GLP-1 race, which I view as very weak and slow.

Therefore, I believe at least ~30% more upside is left here to match high-growth BigPharma's average.

Risks to consider

I've already talked about competition with Novo and the negligence of Lilly's timing disadvantage.

But we also have "dark horses" of GLP-1 (Viking, Amgen, Pfizer, and Structure), which all lack compelling sustainable trial data, with phase III readouts expected only post mid-2027. As many firms are getting ready to jump on this opportunity, the market could price in a possible competition discount in 2026, preparing for the readouts over the next two years.

Additionally, I do see a possibility of a black swan event in regard to retatrutide. Although the main adverse events categories drawbacks are lower than those of CagriSema, the stage IIa drug introduced us to new types of risk.

Retatrutide is associated with significant systolic blood pressure reduction by ~14 mmHg (x2 compared to Stage I drugs), but it comes at an expense of a higher heart rate increase (+6-7 bpm). Reta is also the only GLP-1 candidate that has dysesthesia listed as an AE for ~20%, which is described as "skin pain". We will have to wait for full TRIUMPH-4 publication, although no red flags were flashed in the press release.

These risks fully justify the results in my opinion, but they are new to the regulator, and the FDA may slap Cardiac/Arrhythmia Black Box on Retatrutide along with approval.

If these risks prevail, that would more than justify the current ~1x forward PEG valuation and should drive shares lower, meaning there is a chance I might be missing a good exit point.

Takeaway

Eli Lilly is a very important strategic asset in my portfolio because it is the only firm that can give exposure to the largest market that the pharma industry has ever seen.

2026 will likely get slower for revenue growth due to additional competition, which is reflected in the rating downgrade. However, this would only be a timing issue until Orforglipron and Retatrutide approval and scale-up in late 2026, along with Medicare volume and Jaypirca + Kisunla acceleration.

A forward PEG of ~1x does not reflect this type of expected dominance in key markets, which is why I am not turning neutral and recommend aggressively buying any significant dip similar to the August Orfo-related meltdown.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
